title: Basis- and Channel-Selective Quantum Photodetection
abstract: Photodetection converts optical quantum states into measurement events, but the usual electric-field response model becomes restrictive when the detector response is shaped by cavity, superconducting, or metamaterial engineering. We develop a generalized quantum photodetection framework in which electric and magnetic field amplitudes contribute coherently to the detection operator, and analyze it in a far-field two-source geometry, a two-mode single-photon setting, and a lossy resonant detector model. The far-field reference case exhibits complete detector-amplitude cancellation, absent in the electric-only Glauber response, while the single-photon model shows that the detector continuously rotates the effective measurement basis and controls the first-order visibility via an exact closed-form law. In the resonant realization, a monitored radiative output channel can be dark while the detector remains internally excited and absorptive, with unit absorption of the matched input mode at critical coupling. These results identify basis-selective readout and channel-selective absorption as experimentally relevant signatures of engineered electric-magnetic photodetection.
summary: This paper proposes a generalized theory of photodetection that includes both electric and magnetic field sensitivities. It demonstrates that by engineering these responses, one can actively control the measurement basis of single photons and create detectors that absorb light without emitting detectable radiation. This offers a new design principle for advanced quantum sensors and metamaterial-based detectors.
main_problem: Standard quantum photodetection models (Glauber theory) rely solely on electric-field response, which fails to account for modern engineered detectors that are sensitive to both electric and magnetic fields.
main_result: The authors develop a generalized framework where the detector's electric and magnetic responses can be used to rotate the measurement basis and selectively suppress specific radiative output channels.
method: The study uses an extension of Glauber's theory of optical coherence, POVM formalism, and input-output theory to analyze detection probability and absorption.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to three scenarios: a far-field two-source dipole geometry, a two-mode single-photon Hilbert space, and a lossy resonant detector model with electric and magnetic coupling.
key_observables: Detection probability, first-order visibility, path distinguishability, and absorption fraction.
important_parameters: The complex parameter zeta (ratio of magnetic to electric response), radiative linewidths (gamma_e, gamma_m), and internal decay rate (gamma_i).
main_assumptions: The model is presented as an effective description of engineered electromagnetic responses.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the two-dipole geometry and detection amplitude cancellation; Figure 2 illustrates detector-defined interference, visibility/path-bias relations, and Bloch sphere representation; Figure 3 shows radiative rates and the relationship between output channels and absorption.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a generalized detection operator, applies it to far-field interference, extends the analysis to single-photon basis rotation, investigates resonant absorption and channel selection, and concludes with a design principle for engineered detectors.
why_interesting: It provides a new way to think about detector engineering in quantum optics and circuit QED, showing that the detector architecture itself can be used as a tool to select measurement bases and control light-matter interaction channels.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Two-dipole far-field reference geometry for generalized electric–magnetic photodetection. (a) Two in-phase Hertzian dipoles with moments oriented along ˆz are placed at positions ±d/2 and observed by a far-field detector D. The local detector frame resolves the radiated electric and magnetic components through the generalized detection amplitude b O ∝ue·bE(+) + ζ um·bF(+), with bF(+) = cbB(+). (b,c) Normalized radiative detection probability Pscat for the polar cut ϕ = 0 and the azimuthal cut θ = π/2, respectively, for d = 3λ. The electric-only Glauber response corresponds to ζ = 0, while ζ = 1 gives constructive electric–magnetic addition and ζ = −1 gives complete cancellation of...

Figure 2. Detector-defined single-photon interference in the two-mode geometry. (a) Normalized detection probability Pζ(x)/⟨Pζ⟩x for representative real values of the electric–magnetic response parameter ζ. As ζ approaches the balanced value ζ = 1, the detector becomes sensitive to a single propagation direction and the fringe visibility is suppressed, even though the single-photon state remains coherent. (b) Visibility Vζ and signed path bias Bζ for real ζ. The vertical dashed lines mark ζ = ±1, where the detector selects one of the two propagation directions and the visibility vanishes. The curves satisfy the complementarity relation V2 ζ +|Bζ|2 = 1, showing that the loss of visibility...

Figure 3. Scattering-dark yet absorptive response of a lossy electric–magnetic detector resonance. (a) Bright and dark radiative rates γb and γd as functions of the magnetic-to-electric coupling ratio γm/γe. At the balanced point γm = γe, the dark-channel rate vanishes, γd = 0, while the bright channel remains coupled. (b) Normalized dark-channel output |sout d |2/|sin|2 and resonant absorption A(ω0) for a matched bright input mode. At the balanced point, the monitored dark output is suppressed by destructive electric–magnetic reradiation, whereas the absorption remains finite and reaches unity here because the internal loss is chosen at critical coupling, γi = γr.
